coredata.dc:title: A biocompatible nanoplatform formed by MgAl-layered double hydroxide modified Mn3O4/N-graphene quantum dot conjugated-polyaniline for pH-triggered release of doxorubicin
coredata.dc:creator: Ahmadi-Kashani, Mina
Dehghani, Hossein
Zarrabi, Ali
coredata.dc:description: In this work, for the first time, a novel pH-sensitive biocompatible multifunctional nanocarrier was fabricated by the combination of MgAl-layered double hydroxide, Mn3O4 nanoparticles, N-graphene quantum dot and polyaniline (PANI/N-GQD/MO/LDH) for doxorubicin (DOX) delivery in breast cancer cells. Electrochemical techniques, including cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV), were employed for proving the surface modification process. The integration of polyaniline on the surface of the nanocarrier provides ultrahigh DOX encapsulation up to 90% and possesses a slow-release behavior (4% after 72 h) under normal physiological conditions. However, releasing ~80% of the drug in a low-pH environment as a model of the extracellular tumor environment happened, presenting a pH-triggered release. The cell viability using MTT assay reveals that the DOX/PANI/N-GQD/MO/LDH had no apparent adverse effect on the viability of human L929 normal cells. Furthermore, a significant inhibition ratio against human breast cancer cell lines (MCF˗7) was observed when the cells were treated with the DOX-loaded PANI/N-GQD/MO/LDH nanocarrier, suggesting that this nanocarrier could increase the therapeutic efficacy of DOX. The hemolysis rates (HRs) of human fresh blood, coagulation prothrombin time (PT), activated partial thromboplastin time (APTT), and complement activation (C3 and C4 levels) revealed the excellent blood compatibility of the nanocarrier. Thus, the nano-vehicle designed in this study could be used as a novel multifunctional and synergistic, pH-triggered platform for delivering various anti-cancer drugs and other biomedical applications.

head.ce:title: A biocompatible nanoplatform formed by MgAl-layered double hydroxide modified Mn3O4/N-graphene quantum dot conjugated-polyaniline for pH-triggered release of doxorubicin

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Inorganic Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Kashan, P.O. Box 87317-51167, Kashan, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Inorganic Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Kashan, P.O. Box 87317-51167, Kashan, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Sabanci University Nanotechnology Research and Application Center (SUNUM)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Sabanci University, Nanotechnology Research and Application Center (SUNUM), Tuzla, Istanbul, 34956, Turkey.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, for the first time, a novel pH-sensitive biocompatible multifunctional nanocarrier was fabricated by the combination of MgAl-layered double hydroxide, Mn3O4 nanoparticles, N-graphene quantum dot and polyaniline (PANI/N-GQD/MO/LDH) for doxorubicin (DOX) delivery in breast cancer cells. Electrochemical techniques, including cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV), were employed for proving the surface modification process. The integration of polyaniline on the surface of the nanocarrier provides ultrahigh DOX encapsulation up to 90% and possesses a slow-release behavior (4% after 72 h) under normal physiological conditions. However, releasing ~80% of the drug in a low-pH environment as a model of the extracellular tumor environment happened, presenting a pH-triggered release. The cell viability using MTT assay reveals that the DOX/PANI/N-GQD/MO/LDH had no apparent adverse effect on the viability of human L929 normal cells. Furthermore, a significant inhibition ratio against human breast cancer cell lines (MCF˗7) was observed when the cells were treated with the DOX-loaded PANI/N-GQD/MO/LDH nanocarrier, suggesting that this nanocarrier could increase the therapeutic efficacy of DOX. The hemolysis rates (HRs) of human fresh blood, coagulation prothrombin time (PT), activated partial thromboplastin time (APTT), and complement activation (C3 and C4 levels) revealed the excellent blood compatibility of the nanocarrier. Thus, the nano-vehicle designed in this study could be used as a novel multifunctional and synergistic, pH-triggered platform for delivering various anti-cancer drugs and other biomedical applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Chemotherapy is considered as the widely used therapeutic strategy for cancer [1]. Doxorubicin (DOX) is a family of chemotherapeutic drugs, using against different types of cancers such as breast, stomach, lungs, leukemia, lymphomas, etc. The main mechanism of cancer cell killing by DOX is inhibition of topoisomerase IIβ via chelation of DNA. However, this drug has severe side effects, such as heart damage, hair loss, allergic reactions, and tissue inflammation [2]. Moreover, uncontrolled release rate, short half-life in blood circulation, and low bioavailability are other problems with this drug. Therefore, the fabrication of nanocarriers with an improved half-life of drug, targeted, and selective release and superior biocompatibility remains a challenge for DOX delivery. Nowadays, nanoparticle-based drug delivery systems (DDSs) involving multifunctional components have been widely investigated for cancer treatment due to their improved biodistribution and efficacy, reduced toxicity, and side effects via the enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect [3]. The incorporation of three or more platforms in one system is very interesting due to providing an efficient synergistic treatment to cancer cells [4]. Mainly, stimuli-responsive drug delivery systems are of crucial importance for enhancing the chemotherapy effects. These systems have drug release ability with control of the time, area, and dosage, leading to minimize the adverse effects of drugs by sensing and responding to external stimuli (e.g., temperature, pH, electric or magnetic field, ultrasound, light, etc.) [5]. As known, the pH in the external microenvironment of tumors is more acidic than normal tissues. Therefore, the construction of a pH-responsive carrier can control the site-specific delivery of anticancer drugs well. Using the pH-responsive systems, the drug can release much faster at tumor tissues than the normal one without external stimulus, causing to reduce the side effects of anti-cancer drugs [6].

As one class of inorganic nanoparticles, the layered double hydroxides (LDHs), have particularly attracted extensive considerations as influential drug delivery vehicles because of the inherent properties, such as their low cost, high biocompatibility, nontoxicity, enhanced drug stability and pH-controlled release of guest molecules [7]. In the last few years, MgAl-LDH nanoparticles (NPs), attracted particular attention in biomedical applications. Maiti et al. developed a series of magnesium aluminum layered double hydroxides, and raloxifene drug was introduced within the layers [8]. The contemporary approach for delivery of siRNA and anti-cancer drug 5-fluorouracil (5-FU) by Mg2Al-LDH-Cl NPs was employed by Gu and Xu's group. The result displayed that LDHs are a proper candidate to conquer drug resistance and improve cancer therapy [9]. Ghosh et al. reported anticancerous drug methotrexate (MTX) incorporated into MgAl layered double hydroxide. The LDH-MTX showed a strikingly higher sensitivity/efficacy of the drug on colon cancer [10]. Nevertheless, owing to the easy aggregations of LDHs in culture solution or PBS, the use of LDH NPs in vivo is still restricted. Besides, the release of drugs from LDHs is found to be very quick. To overcome these limitations, surface modification of LDHs is one of the proper solutions. The incorporation of metal oxides with LDH can reduce the burst drug release effect and prolong the release time of the drug. Among different types of metal oxide nanoparticles, hausmannite (Mn3O4) has been expansively employed due to its low cost, environmental compatibility and wide-ranging applications in various fields, such as pharmaceutical industries, magnetic resonance contrast agents, fluorescence imaging of cancer cells, as an intracellular drug delivery platform and so on [11]. However, the agglomeration of Mn3O4 NPs is one of the crucial challenges, resulting in a reduced surface area and limits the applications of these NPs in drug delivery. The integration of N-graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) with nanocarrier is greatly desirable to overcome this problem and achieve better anti-cancer efficiency. Similar to graphene nanosheets, N-GQDs display various exceptional characteristics such as high surface area, the outstanding capability of drug loading, and versatile surface functionalization by using the π–π conjugated network [12]. So, N-GQD is one of the most potential ideal constituents to construct a synergistic platform with LDH and Mn3O4 NPs [13]. The N-GQD can incorporate with Mn3O4/LDH through interaction between nitrogen-containing functional groups of N-GQD and the surface of hydroxyl/carboxyl groups of the layers. The properties of Mn3O4/LDH, together with the N-GQD, can provide higher surface area, more active site for drug encapsulation and a slower and further continuous release mode of drugs. Meanwhile, the N-GQD hinders the agglomeration of LDH and Mn3O4 NPs and improves their practical applications as a carrier [14]. The incorporation of polymer with nanocarriers can provide more biocompatibility, enhanced permanence, and functional groups for the interconnection of drugs [15]. Among the conducting polymers, polyaniline (PANI) NPs have attracted tremendous attention because of the unique π-conjugated structure, their chemical stability, good biocompatibility, conductivity, mechanical flexibility, and low cost [16]. Besides, polyaniline is non-cytotoxic and it is considered as an ideal candidate for the development of drug delivery systems [17]. The combination of PANI with LDH, Mn3O4, and N-GQD are expected to be highly effective in the improvement of biocompatibility of nanocarriers. Besides, the incorporation of PANI with nanocarrier provides a multifunctional system with the higher surface area for improved loading and selective release, the extended half-life of DOX, and detectable slower DOX release, enhancing the anti-cancer drug performance and reducing side effects to normal cells. Because of the abundant amino and carboxyl groups on the surfaces of the N-CQDs, it is expected that the PANI covalently embedded with the N-CQDs, or adsorbed by electrostatic interaction or hydrogen bond.

Herein, we developed a novel and interesting pH-responsive anti-cancer drug delivery of MgAl–LDH modified polyaniline/N-GQD/Mn3O4 nanocomposite incorporated with the chemotherapeutic agent (DOX), aiming to obtain a biocompatible drug delivery system with ultrahigh drug storage capacity, controlled drug release, decrease drug adverse side effects and better anti-cancer efficiency. Modification of Mn3O4/LDH with N-GQD and polyaniline lead to ultrahigh encapsulation of DOX, displaying a higher value than the previous studies [18–22]. This nanocomposite can considerably overcome drug-resistance by a quick release of the drug under acidic pH environment about 80%. Moreover, this nanoplatform possesses a slow-release behavior that tolerates drug to be administered over prolonged periods. To the best of our knowledge, there is no work previously reported with LDH modified with polyaniline/N-GQD/Mn3O4 for DOX delivery. The surface modification process probed with cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) techniques. MTT assay was carried out to evaluate the cell viabilities of the samples. The nanocarriers had no obvious adverse effect on cell viability towards L929, as normal cells, even at a high dosage of 100 mg mL−1. Furthermore, a significant inhibition ratio against MCF-7, as breast cancer-cells, was observed when the cells were treated with the DOX-loaded PANI/N-GQD/MO/LDH nanocarrier. Up to now, only a few efforts have been devoted to investigating the interaction of DOX-loaded nanocarriers with blood and its constituents. In this work, hemolytic behavior of nano-vehicles, coagulation prothrombin time (PT) and activated partial thromboplastin time (APTT) as blood biocompatibility were applied and these tests revealed the excellent blood compatibility of nanocarrier and complement activation (C3 and C4 levels) showed this functional nanocomposite could be appropriate for biological applications.

All chemicals used in this study were of pure analytical grade and used without further purification. Doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX) was provided by EBEWE Pharmaceutical Company (Unterach, AUSTRIA). Magnesium nitrate hexahydrate (Mg(NO3)2·6H2O), aluminum nitrate nonahydrate (Al(NO3)3·9H2O), sodium carbonate, sodium hydroxide (NaOH), aniline, acid chloride, potassium persulfate (KPS), citric acid, urea, thiourea, manganese sulfate monohydrate, and sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) were purchased from Merck. Deionized water was used as a solvent. The phosphate buffer saline (PBS, 0.1 M) was applied as a buffer solution (pH = 7.4), and the acetate buffer solution (pH 5.0) was prepared by adjusting the sodium acetate and acetic acid salts (Merck).

Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were carried out on a Phillips X'Pert PRO X-ray diffractometer (XRD) equipped with graphite monochromatized CuKα radiation having a scanning range of 0–80 Bragg's angle. UV–vis spectra of the solutions were measured on a UV 1800 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded with a Magna 550 Nicolet FTIR instrument, in the spectral range between 4000 and 400 cm−1 using the KBr pellet technique. The morphologies of the synthesized products were observed directly by the TESCAN BRNO-Mira3 LMU field emission scanning electron microscope (FE˗SEM) equipped with energy dispersive X-ray (EDS). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed for the nanoparticle characterization using (TEM EM208S PHILIPS). The hydrodynamic size distribution and zeta potential of samples were determined by dynamic light scattering (DLS) using a particle size analyzer (Microtrac Nano W3776). Electrochemical measurements such as cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) (Sama 500 potentiostat (Isfahan, Iran)) were performed with a conventional three-electrode system consisted of a GCE (working), platinum wire (counter) and Ag/AgCl (reference) electrodes in 2 M KOH solution. Activated partial prothrombin time (APTT) and prothrombin time (PT) were measured as parameters of blood compatibility using (STAR Evolution, Diagnostica Stago, Assiernes, France). A Metrohm model 691 pH/mV meter was also used for pH measurements.

A layered double hydroxide (MgAl-CO3 LDH) was prepared by a co-precipitation reaction at constant pH with the following procedure. Briefly, Mg(NO3)2·6H2O (0.01 mol) and Al(NO3)3·9H2O (0.005 mol) (molar ratio of Mg2+/Al3+ = 2/1), were dissolved in 30 mL deionized water. Then, the procedure was completed by concurrent adding NaOH solution (1 mol L−1) and Na2CO3 (0.5 mol L−1) at continuous pH (=10). The resulting suspension was kept under nitrogen flow to remove CO2 and magnetically stirred for 24 h at room temperature. The solid product was removed by centrifugation, and washed with deionized water for several times until pH ~7.0 and dried at 60 °C overnight.

Mn3O4 was synthesized by a simple chemical precipitation method at room temperature. Typically, 5.0 mmol of MnSO4·H2O was dissolved in 50 mL of deionized water containing 10 mmol of NaOH under constant stirring at room temperature. To the above reaction mixture, 1 mg SDS added as surfactant. After 24 h, the brown color precipitate was attained that showed the accomplishment of the reaction. Subsequently, the obtained product was washed with deionized water several times and dried at 60 °C overnight. The surfactant SDS was removed by thermal treatment of as-prepared sample by calcination at 500 °C for 2 h under air.

Polyaniline synthesized by in situ chemical-oxidative polymerization of aniline using KPS as oxidant [23]. In a typical procedure, 0.1 mL aniline was dispersed in 10 mL HCl (1 M) by ultrasonication for 15 min. Then, another solution of 0.3 g KPS in 10 mL HCl (1 M) was rapidly added to the monomeric solution under vigorous stirring in an ice-water bath. The color of the mixture changed into the green after about 5 min, indicating polymerization of aniline. After stirring at room temperature overnight, the reaction mixture consisting of a dark-green suspension of PANI was filtered, washed with ethanol and DI water, and then dried at 60 °C.

For the synthesis of N-GQD, 1 mmol of citric acid and 3 mmol of urea were dissolved into 15 mL water, and stirred to form a clear solution. Then, the solution was transferred into Teflon lined stainless autoclave, and it was heated to 180 °C for 12 h by hydrothermal treatment. Subsequently, the N-GQDs were collected by filtration through a 0.22 μm Teflon membrane.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of DOX loaded nanocarrier and in vitro release study
ce:section.ce:para:
0.01 g of LDH was dispersed in 10 mL of DI. Next, 0.004 g of Mn3O4 NPs was added to the solution. After ultrasonication for 5 min, 0.5 mL N-GQD and 0.002 g PANI were added to the above-mixed solution. The samples were then sonicated for 15 min, followed by the addition of 2.8 mL DOX (2 mg mL−1) to the solution. Subsequently, the solution was stirred at dark for 24 h at room temperature. The DOX-loaded PANI/N-GQD/MO/LDH nanocomposites were collected by centrifugation and were washed three times with DI to remove the physically adsorbed drug molecules. All the supernatant solution was collected together and measured by a UV–Vis spectrophotometer at a fixed wavelength (480 nm) to determine the quantity of DOX loading in the PANI/N-GQD/MO/LDH nanocomposite. The PANI/N-GQD/MO/LDH nanocomposite was prepared according to the above procedure without adding DOX. The different steps for the fabrication of DOX-loaded PANI/N-GQD/MO/LDH nanocomposites are shown in Fig. 1 . The DOX-loading capacity and the encapsulation efficiency were calculated based on Eqs. (1) and (2) [24].

The pH-dependent drug release studies were performed under reservoir-sink conditions. The drug-loaded PANI/N-GQD/MO/LDH (3 mg) was dispersed into 2 ml of release medium (acetate buffer-pH 5, PBS pH 7.4) and then put into a dialysis bag. The dialysis was employed against 30 ml of phosphate buffer saline (PBS-pH 7.4) and acetate buffer-pH 5 under constant stirring at 37 °C to mimic the cellular and external tumor environment, respectively. 2 ml of the external medium was withdrawn and replaced with fresh medium at interval times to preserve sink situations. The amount of released DOX in the supernatant was determined by UV–Vis spectrophotometer at λexcitation = 480 nm. The percentage of drug release was calculated utilizing Eq. (3):
(3)
Drug release
%
=
Drug release concentration Drug loading concentration
× 100

Human breast cancer cell lines (MCF7) and human L929 fibroblasts cells were grown as an adherent monolayer in RPMI 1640 (Biowhittaker, Belgium) in DMEM (Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium, Gibco). The medium complemented with 10% Fetal Bovine Serum (FBS; Sigma-Aldrich, USA), 100 μg ml−1 streptomycin, and 100 Units ml−1 penicillin at 37 °C in 5% CO2.

MTT method was used as an assay for the determination of in vitro cytotoxicity in MCF-7 and L929 cell lines using four different concentrations of samples (25, 50, 75, and 100 μg/mL) to check the toxicity of the nanocarriers [25]. In brief, well-growing MCF-7 and L929 cell lines were seeded in 96-well plates (4000 cells per well), and the plate was incubated in a CO2 incubator (5% CO2/95% O2) at 37 °C. After 24 h, various concentrations of NPs without and with DOX loading were added into each well incubated for 24 h, 48 h, and 72 h. After an appropriate time, the cell culture media of each well was removed, cells washed with PBS, and then 100 μL fresh media along with 10 μL MTT solution (5 mg mL−1 in PBS) was added to each well followed by incubation for another 4 h. Next, the supernatant was exchanged by 100 μl DMSO and incubated for another 1 h to dissolve the formazan pigment produced by living cells. The absorbance of the suspension was measured at 493 nm by an ELISA reader (Biorad-USA). The cell viability was calculated using Eq. (4):
(4)

%
=
OD

OD

× 100
where OD treated was obtained from the cells treated by a specific agent under various conditions, and cells without treatment were used as OD control [26].
To evaluate the blood compatibility tests, blood samples were collected from healthy volunteer adhering to ethical principles.

The anti-coagulated blood sample (3 ml) was centrifuged to separate the red blood cells, washed with PBS for three times, and the supernatant was separated from pellets. The 200 μl of washed blood and 4 mL PBS were mixed for further dilution. Then, the diluted blood suspension (200 μl) was added to 800 μL of different concentrations of DOX-loaded PANI/N-GQD/MO/LDH. The mixture was retained at room temperature for 2 h, and then the mixtures were centrifuged at 1600 rpm for 5 min. Deionized water and PBS were used as a positive and negative control, respectively. The supernatant fluid was read at 541 nm using a spectrophotometric to determine the hemolysis. The hemolysis percentage was calculated from the following Eq. (5):
(5)
Percent Hemolysis
%
=
D s − D n
D p − D n
× 100
where Ds, Dn, and Dp were the absorbance of the sample, negative control, and the positive control, respectively [27]. The values reported are the means of triplicate measurements.

Blood coagulation contains several complex biochemical reactions leading to the construction of a fibrin clot. The samples were tested for their possible impacts on blood coagulation time. Prothrombin time (PT) and Partial prothrombin time (APTT), as parameters of blood biocompatibility, were chosen to assess the time taken for the blood clotting [28,29]. They were measured by an automated blood coagulation analyzer, using mechanical end-point determination (ST4, Diagnostica Stago). For the PT determination, the time that the sample takes to clot was measured via activation of the extrinsic coagulation pathway by Innovin® reagent when incubated with plasma. For the Partial prothrombin time or activated partial thromboplastin time (APTT) determination, the intrinsic and common coagulation pathways were stimulated by the addition of actin as partial thromboplastin reagent and calcium chloride to plasma. Then, the time was measured to the point of a clot formation. The samples effect on coagulation performance was studied by addition of plasma to different concentrations of DOX/PANI/N-GQD/MO/LDH (25, 50, 75 and 100 μg ml−1) and PANI/N-GQD/MO/LDH (100 μg ml−1) solution in isotonic saline (9:1 dilution plasma: sample solution). For control experiments, identical volumes of plasma were added to the saline solution without any nanocarriers. Before applying the coagulation reagents, the samples mixed at 37 °C for 5 min. For control experiments, identical quantities of isotonic saline solution were added to the plasma. Experiments were repeated three times.

C3 and C4 as proteins are vital factors in the activation of the complement system. These proteins are part of the complement system. The complementary system consists of nearly 60 proteins that exist in blood plasma and on the surface of some cells. C3 and C4 relate to the immune system and have a substantial role in protecting the body from infections, dead cells, and foreign materials. Levels of C3 and C4 in the blood can be considered as a particular medical diagnosis. To measure the complement activation, the cleavage of complement constituents, including complement component 3 (C3) and complement component 4 (C4) as proteins related to the immune system, were examined with a commercial single radial immuno-diffusion (SRID) immunoassay kit [30]. Complement activation analyses were completed by anti-coagulated plasma (plasma in 3.8% sodium citrate). The plasma and the sample solutions were mixed in isotonic saline to the final concentrations of 25, 50, 70, and 100 μg ml−1. The mixture of plasma and saline solution without any sample was used as the control. After 2 h incubation at 37 °C, the samples were removed. Ultimately, all samples were invoked to one well of an SRID plate based on the manufacturer's instructions. The base function of SRID is on the connection of the particular antibodies covered on the plate and C3/C4 in the samples producing an observable precipitate in relation to their concentrations. Next, zone diameters were measured after the sample used in agreement with the manufacturer's suggestion for 48 h. The biocompatibility index is described by differences between C3/C4 concentrations in the samples and the control. The values expressed are the means of triplicate measurements.

All measurable data were examined by SPSS software (version 21). Data are reported as mean values ± standard deviation (SD), and the statistical differences were measured to be significant at a level of P < 0.05. The parametric analysis of variance [ANOVA (Tukey)] was applied for statistical evaluations of the data.

EDS analysis (Fig. 2 ) was applied to determine the chemical composition of the prepared samples. It is seen that the as-prepared MgAl-CO3 LDH consisted of Mg, Al, O, and C without any impurities (Fig. 2a). The EDS pattern of the Mn3O4 NPs was presented in Fig. 2b. The peaks at 0.5, 5.9, and 6.5 keV correspond to manganese, while the peak at 0.48 keV belongs to oxygen [31]. The EDS spectrum of PANI/N-GQD/MO/LDH nanocomposite was shown in Fig. 2c, which indicates the presence of Mg, Al, C, O, Mn, and N. The nitrogen peak is attributed to the nitrogen-containing functional groups in PANI and N-GQD. It should be noted that no additional peaks attributed to contaminations or impurities were identified, demonstrating the purity of the obtained products.

The purity and crystalline features of the as-prepared samples were characterized using XRD (Fig. 3 ). In the case of LDH, the diffraction peaks at 2θ = 11.83°, 23.60°, 34.79°, 35.80°, 39.19°, 46.52°, 61.02°, 62.34°, and 66.31° can be indexed as the pure hexagonal phase of MgAl-LDH (JCPDS No. 20-0658). For Mn3O4, the diffraction peaks, which appeared in the XRD pattern, were indexed using the JCPDS card No. 89-4837. No other crystalline impurities were observed by XRD, revealing the high purity of the prepared Mn3O4 NPs. The XRD pattern of as-synthesized PANI shows broad characteristic reflection peaks located at 2θ = 25.2° and a weak diffraction peak at 20.3°, which is ascribed to the periodicity parallel and the periodicity perpendicular to the polymer chain, respectively [32]. The XRD pattern of PANI/N-GQD/MO/LDH contains the diffraction peaks of both LDH and Mn3O4 nanoparticles. Moreover, it shows that the growth of PANI/N-GQD/MO on the MgAl-LDH has no apparent effect on the phase structure of surface-modified LDH. Besides, the peak intensities of PANI/N-GQD/MO/LDH are stronger than that of MgAl-LDH, and pure Mn3O4, and the characteristic peaks corresponding to PANI and N-GQD are absent in the case of the nanocomposite. This might be due to the successful incorporation of PANI/N-GQD in the Mn3O4/LDH. As compared to the PANI/N-GQD/MO/LDH, after the intercalation of DOX inside the layers of LDH, the intensity of diffraction peaks becomes stronger. These results display that DOX has been effectively introduced into the LDH lamellar.

The FTIR spectra of the LDH, Mn3O4, PANI, N-GQD, Mn3O4/LDH, PANI/N-GQD/MO/LDH and the DOX/PANI/N-GQD/MO/LDH are shown in Fig. 4 . The FTIR spectrum of LDH displays the characteristic lattice vibration bands in the 450˗800 cm−1 region that could be attributed to the M–O and O-M-O vibration modes. The wavenumber value of 1358 cm−1 can ascribe to the asymmetric stretching vibration of the interlayer carbonates. A broad band appears at 3434 cm−1, which is due to the OH stretching of the hydroxyl group and water molecules existing in the interlayer space of the LDH. The as-prepared Mn3O4 NPs show characteristic bands (in the range of 400–900 cm−1) at 606 and 494 cm−1, which are related to the stretching vibrations of MnO and Mn–O–Mn bonds. The spectrum of PANI displays four peaks at 1297, 1241, 1113, and 796 cm−1 corresponding to the strong aromatic CN stretching vibrations, –C=N stretching vibrations, the N=Q=N (Q represents the quinoid ring) stretching and the aromatic C–H out-of-plane bending vibration, respectively [33]. The vibrational peak around 3400 cm−1 is assigned to NH stretching vibrations. The peaks found at 1561 and 1476 cm−1 are identified as the vibration modes of the CC quinoid ring and benzenoid structure of the PANI, respectively. For N-GQD, the stretching vibrations of the amino and hydroxyl display the broad bands at 3000–3500 cm−1. The peak at 1401 cm−1 could be ascribed to the symmetric stretching vibration of CN, demonstrating successful doping of nitrogen atom [34]. The sharp bands at around 1603 cm−1 are attributed to the CC stretching modes of aromatic hydrocarbons. For Mn3O4/LDH, all representative peaks in the case of LDH and Mn3O4 were also observed. In comparison to Mn3O4/LDH, new peaks emerge in the PANI/N-GQD/MO/LDH. The sharp peak at 3433 cm−1 is related to –OH/N–H groups stretching vibration. The characteristic bands at 1631, and 1382 cm−1 are corresponding to the amide CONH and the stretching vibration modes of C-NH [35], which demonstrate the successful intercalation of PANI and N-GQD into the MgAl-layered double hydroxide. For the spectrum of the DOX/PANI/N-GQD/MO/LDH, all bands exhibited shift to the lower wavenumbers as compared to the PANI/N-GQD/MO/LDH. This spectral variation suggests not only the presence of hydrogen bond and π-π interactions among DOX, PANI, and N-GQDs, but also indicates the successful formation of the DOX/PANI/N-GQD/MO/LDH nanocomposite.

The cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) were performed at room temperature and in a 2 M KOH aqueous electrolyte to characterize the surface modification of LDH and confirm conjunction of DOX on the nanocomposite surface. A three-electrode system was prepared for electrochemical measurements with a platinum wire as a counter electrode, an Ag/AgCl, and a glassy carbon electrode (3 mm diameter) as a reference and working electrode, respectively. The samples were dropped onto the glassy carbon electrode after ultrasonically redispersed in DI. As shown in Fig. 5a, under the influence of applied voltage and with a 100 mVs−1 scan rate, no obvious redox peak was observed for pure LDH, and with the integration of the Mn3O4 NPs onto the LDH surface, a pair of redox peak detected. The appearance of the redox peak is related to the reaction between the manganese ions and the electrolyte. There was an increase in redox current response in order of DOX/PANI/N-GQD/MO/LDH > PANI/N-GQD/MO/LDH > N-GQD/MO/LDH > MO/LDH > LDH, which could be attributed to the synergistic effect of individual constituents, excellent conductivity of PANI and N-GQD and larger surface area provided by attachments of samples on the LDH surface, facilitating electron transfer at the surface of the electrodes. The obtained DPV results (Fig. 5b) for the samples prove that the current response of each product is the same as the increment in the current of electrodes measured by CV. Therefore, these observations indicate successful modification of LDH and immobilization of DOX on the surface of the nanocomposite.

FE˗SEM images were used to evaluate the surface morphology of the LDH, Mn3O4, PANI, N-GQD NPs, the PANI/N-GQD/MO/LDH nanocomposite and the DOX/PANI/N-GQD/MO/LDH. It is apparent from the Fig. 6a that, the LDH samples consist of non-porous plate-like particles with an almost hexagonal shape. The as-obtained Mn3O4 exposed the accumulation of tiny particles with irregular shape of larger particles. (Fig. 6b). The FE˗SEM image of PANI was displayed in Fig. 6c. In terms of this image, the PANI is combinations of smaller particles with a closely assembled network, showing a porous structure and providing a high surface area that can improve the drug loading capacity. It is observed in Fig. 6d that the synthesized N-GQD displays spherical agglomerated NPs with the narrow and homogeneous distribution. As the LDH modified with MO, N-GQD, and PANI, the size and thickness of LDH layers changed, and the particles distributed regularly on the LDH surface (Fig. 6e). The FE˗SEM image of the DOX/PANI/N-GQD/MO/LDH sample (Fig. 6f) shows, the particles are closely and continuously aggregated because of the irregular plate–plate stacking compare to bare LDH and the strong particle-particle interaction among small particles of MO, PANI, N-GQD, and DOX, proving the successful attachment of drug into the nano-platform. To study more on the morphology of the DOX/PANI/N-GQD/MO/LDH, TEM images were examined (Fig. 6g). It is obvious; the layered structure of the LDH is still preserved, but the regular hexagon shape of the LDH layers changes to ellipse and extensive agglomeration observed. The high intensity of black dots shows that NPs successfully incorporated on the LDH surface. The particle size distribution and zeta potential were measured using dynamic light scattering (DLS) (Fig. 7 ). From DLS measurements, it was determined that the MO/LDH nanocomposite has an average particle size of about 243 nm (Fig. 7a). With further modification with the N-GQD and polyaniline (PANI/N-GQD/MO/LDH), the average particle size decreased to 114 nm (Fig. 7b). A low polydispersity index (PDI) of 0.184 and 0.138 obtained by DLS analysis for the MO/LDH and the PANI/N-GQD/MO/LDH, respectively, indicating good dispersibility of the samples. The LDH NPs showed high zeta-potential (+38 mV), suggesting the positive charge of the LDH surfaces [36]. The high charge of LDH makes them simply attach to the negative surface of the samples. The incorporation of Mn3O4 NPs and N-GQD decreased the zeta-potential value from +38 mV of LDH to −18.8 mV, which is probably associated with the formation of negative charges on the LDH surface. Moreover, when the polyaniline was added into the LDH, the zeta potential slightly reduced from −18.8 mV to −22.8 mV due to π-π stacking interaction among PANI and N-GQD and creation of partially negative charges on the external surfaces of LDH sample. This phenomenon provided further evidence that PANI and N-GQD were successfully embedded on the LDH surface.

The results of encapsulation efficiency and loading capacity for all of the samples illustrated in Table 1 . The interaction of DOX with LDH and Mn3O4/LDH is assumed to be through the electrostatic interactions [37]. Owing to the plentiful functional groups on the surfaces of the N-GQD/Mn3O4/LDH and PANI/N-GQD/Mn3O4/LDH nanocarriers such as the hydroxyl/carboxyl groups of the layers, nitrogen and oxygen-containing functional groups on the N-GQD, existence of π-conjugation system in N-GQD and PANI, enormous quantities of amine and imine functional groups on the surface of PANI, the loading of DOX onto N-GQD/Mn3O4/LDH and PANI/N-GQD/Mn3O4/LDH could be attributed to various interactions among LDH, Mn3O4, N-GQD, PANI, and DOX such as hydrogen bonding, π-π stacking, and electrostatic interactions [15,38,39]. It was notable that the PANI/N-GQD/MO/LDH offered higher drug encapsulation and loading than other samples (90.4% and 25.3%, respectively) due to the stronger interactions between drug and this nanocarrier. Actually, the embedment of PANI on the nanocarrier provided the more active sites and surface area for DOX attachment. The drug loading capacity of PANI/N-GQD/MO/LDH displayed a higher value than the previous studies [22,40,41]. These results showed a high potential of the PANI/N-GQD/MO/LDH for the loading of DOX with outstandingly high encapsulation efficiency.

The pH-triggered release of DOX from the nanocarriers was investigated in PBS (pH 7.4) and acetate buffer (pH 5) as a pH model of blood and the extracellular tumor environment, respectively for time intervals of up to 72 h. Fig. 8 shows the release of free DOX and drug-loaded LDH, MO/LDH, N-GQD/MO/LDH, and PANI/N-GQD/MO/LDH nanocomposite at pH = 7.4. Free DOX exhibited a high burst release within 12 h, with almost 100% release. In vitro DOX release profiles from the nanocarriers were nonlinear and function of time with a relatively fast release in the initial step and with a sustained release in the second step. A burst drug release (up to 30% in 4 h) was detected for DOX/LDH due to the DOX adsorbed onto the LDH surfaces or weak-bonded loaded DOX molecules. The integration of Mn3O4 and N-GQD on the LDH surfaces can act as a barrier for DOX release. It could be observed that the release of DOX from the MO/LDH and the N-GQD/MO/LDH was 30% and 12% at pH = 7.4, respectively. It is seen that the detachment rate of the DOX from PANI/N-GQD/MO/LDH was slower than other samples and released in a more extended period of time. The releasing ratio of about 4% within 72 h, at pH = 7.4, indicating that the release of DOX from the DOX/PANI/N-GQD/MO/LDH nanocarrier was very sluggish in a normal biological situation without initial burst release in the system and lead to good stability of the drug in the nanocarrier and closely no side effects by controlling of the drug over prolonged periods. Actually, at normal physiological conditions (pH 7.4), the most of DOX will remain in the PANI/N-GQD/MO/LDH nanocomposite for a substantially long time period due to the stronger interaction between drug and nanocarrier, meaning the possibility of continued DOX retention time in the body fluid and thus significantly will be beneficial to effective cancer treatment. The results revealed the improved half-life of DOX compared to the previous research [42–45]. Also, the release behavior of the nanocarriers was investigated at the pH = 5. DOX release from nanocarriers accelerated in response to acidic pH. It could be mainly attributed to the pH-sensitive properties of nanocarriers and the protonation and solubility of the drug that occurs at this low pH [46], leading to the detachment of the chemisorbed drug molecules and destruction of hydrogen bonding, the electrostatic interaction, and π-π stacking interaction between DOX and nanocarrier. It is clearly seen that at pH = 5, the detachment rate of DOX from nanocarriers was in the order of DOX/PANI/N-GQD/MO/LDH > DOX/N-GQD/MO/LDH > DOX/MO/LDH > DOX/LDH, which could be attributed to the more pH-sensitive groups on the surface of nanocarriers. The drug-loaded PANI/N-GQD/MO/LDH released about 80% in acetate buffer-pH 5 media after 72 h, which is much higher than that at pH 7.4. These results are comparable to previous reports [1,22,47]. In other words, the DOX/PANI/N-GQD/MO/LDH nanocomposite could adjust the drug release significantly, and the release of the loaded drug can be turned “off” in blood circulation and “on” in the tumor environment. The pH-dependent release behavior is desirable for assistance in cancer treatment (a slightly acidic environment in tumor lead to stimulating the DOX release in the target tissue to kill cancer cells effectively).

Fig. 9a shows the dose-dependent cytotoxic effect on the L929 cell line after 24 h, 48 h, and 72 h of treatment with the samples. The results show that the PANI/N-GQD/MO/LDH and the DOX/PANI/N-GQD/MO/LDH had no apparent adverse effect on the viability of L929 cells even at a high concentration (100 mg mL−1) after 72 h. Furthermore, the significant inhibition ratio against MCF˗7 cells was observed when the cells were treated with the DOX-loaded PANI/N-GQD/MO/LDH nanocarrier (Fig. 9b). Besides, with increasing of the nanocarrier concentration, the viability of MCF˗7 cells decreased so that it reached to 70 and 60% at the concentrations of 75 and 100 μg/mL, respectively. Compared with the DOX/PANI/N-GQD/MO/LDH nanocomposite, the free DOX showed a higher cytotoxicity. DOX loaded nanocarrier can internalize by endocytosis, and then DOX released slowly over prolonged periods of time, leading to a reduction of dosage and DOX side effects in the body and the prevention of drug resistance in the tumor cells. The result of cytotoxicity studies was calculated according to the Tukey test by statistical analysis (SPSS), and the desired values are identified by “*” in the figures. The statistical changes were measured to be significant at a level of P < 0.05.

To further evaluation of nanocarrier's biocompatibility, the influence of DOX/PANI/N-GQD/MO/LDH on the hemolysis rates (HRs) of human fresh blood was also investigated (Fig. 10 ). Since damage to red blood cells (RBCs) membranes lead to releasing of red hemoglobin, the absorption values of hemoglobin at 541 nm were measured to determine the amount of RBCs hemolysis [48,49]. Phosphate buffered saline (PBS) and deionized water were used as negative and positive controls, respectively. It could be seen that the hemolytic activity was concentration-dependent. Although, the hemolysis rate of the DOX/PANI/N-GQD/MO/LDH has amplified with increasing concentrations (from 25 to 100 μg mL−1), but nanocarrier can be considered blood compatible due to the HRs values were all below 10.0% [22].

The PANI/N-GQD/MO/LDH (100 μg mL−1) and DOX/PANI/N-GQD/MO/LDH samples with different concentrations (25, 50, 75, and 100 μg mL−1) were carried out for investigation of their coagulation effects by examining their capability to change the blood coagulation times, in vitro. It could be seen in Fig. 11 that even at high concentration (100 μg mL−1), PT and APTT results had no noticeable difference with the control PBS that confirmed the samples do not interfere with the coagulation process.

The PANI/N-GQD/MO/LDH (100 μg mL−1) and DOX/PANI/N-GQD/MO/LDH samples with different concentrations (25, 50, 75, and 100 μg mL−1) were incubated with anti-coagulated plasma (plasma in 3.8% sodium citrate) at 37 °C for 2 h, and the amount of C3/C4 formed was examined (Fig. 12 ). The plasma added to isotonic saline was considered as a negative control. Comparison with the control sample, the amount of C3/C4 produced by the influence of nanocarrier did not show significantly change even at a high concentration of 100 μg ml−1, suggesting this nanocomposite has good compatibility with the complement system.

A pH-triggered controlled chemotherapeutic delivery was designed by the modification of MgAl-layered double hydroxide with Mn3O4 NPs coated with polyaniline/N-graphene quantum dot (PANI/N-GQD/MO/LDH). This drug delivery system provides a unique platform for DOX delivery owing to the high number of functional groups and specific surface area. Electrochemical techniques, including cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV), were applied for proving the surface modification process. Results evidently proved that the embedment of polyaniline on the surface of nanocarrier could be beneficial in improving the DOX encapsulation capacity, prolonging drug release at neutral pH, good biocompatibility, high storage stability, and targeting capability. In comparison with the pure LDH, Mn3O4/LDH and N-GQD/Mn3O4/LDH, the multifunctional nanocarrier of PANI/N-GQD/MO/LDH provides ultrahigh encapsulation of DOX up to 90% and drug-releasing of about ~80% in a low-pH environment. The most of DOX will remain in PANI/N-GQD/MO/LDH nanocomposite for a substantially long time at neutral pH (4% for 72 h), proving the sustained DOX retention time in the blood fluids. This pH-dependent release behavior is favorable for increasing cancer treatment efficiency and decreasing the side effects. The cytocompatibility of nanocarriers was evaluated by MTT assay as an in vitro test. The results showed that the nanocarriers presented no significant toxicity at concentrations up to 100 μg mL−1 after incubation with L929 cells. Moreover, after DOX loaded with nanocarrier, up to 40% of cancer cells were killed, suggesting that the PANI/N-GQD/MO/LDH could increase the therapeutic efficacy of DOX. Hemolysis assay demonstrated that DOX loaded nanocarrier had no adverse effects on the red blood cells. Moreover, in vitro blood compatibility examinations of coagulation and complement activation revealed the good blood biocompatibility of nanocarrier. Consequently, the prepared multifunctional nanocarrier displayed great potential for the development of new tools towards cancer therapy and targeted drug delivery.
Mina Ahmadi-Kashani: Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing, Investigation. Hossein Dehghani: Supervision. Ali Zarrabi: Resources, Validation.
